author: @beamer.electronics
text: Curious video, thank you. When watching a film, I love suspending my disbelief, I hate it when a wise guy nearby starts up with - "that doesn't look right?" or "that wouldn't happen in real life?". I've paid good money for my disbelief suspension ;) In saying that though, some films are so far out there, for me - they are almost unwatchable.

author: @sunset1sunset1
text: Like my third grade teacher used to tell me pay attention. 😉 I go out of my way every time I see one of your videos to flag it and try and catch up with it when I get time you do what I dream to do I'm more mechanical electrical but I'm missing so much physics so I love to watch what you do and the way that you explain it the graphene stuff you make complex things simple I just wanted to say thanks and if you're a dad next weekend Happy Father's Day you made me smile thank you

author: @mrdewilliams
text:
I've always had to rely on a form of mitosis, but end up re-merging after only 20-30 minutes of work getting done.  Leaves me feeling twice as ready for a nap too.  Your method seems so much easier.  I'll have to give it a go.

Really though, the first thing that came into my head when I started the video was that Michael Keaton movie, "Multiplicity".  I am going to have to re-watch that one now.  Thanks for a good chuckle.  Brightened up my afternoon.

replies.author: @jimadams7765
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes. That was rather my point too - seeing something from the get go that was completely unbelievable (but possible). As opposed to Rob 2/3, which was "believable" but utterly impossible. And then we've got Quantum Mechanics telling us to believe in the impossible even though we can't see any of it at all at all.
